title: Cataloging Growth: A Re-Evaluation of 1900–1990 - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: MIT economists built an LLM-derived, quality-adjusted price index from 5.1 million Sears catalog listings spanning 1900-1990, finding US real goods consumption grew nearly 4x faster than official deflators imply -- with the biggest gap before World War II, overturning the standard view that pre-1945 growth was slower.

themes.theme: A new LLM-built historical price index

insights.claim: MIT economists Verónica Bäcker-Peral and Benjamin Wittenbrink constructed a new quality-adjusted US consumer price index using 5.1 million product listings from Sears catalogs spanning 1900 to 1990.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A new, large, historically deep dataset for re-benchmarking long-run US inflation and growth estimates -- relevant to anyone using pre-1990 GDP/CPI series as a baseline.
insights.locator: We construct a new quality-adjusted price index

insights.claim: The methodology uses large language models to extract product information and estimate hedonic price schedules from high-dimensional text embeddings, enabling systematic quality adjustment where none existed for most of the twentieth century.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a generalizable LLM-based method for re-measuring historical economic statistics from unstructured archival text/catalog data -- a template applicable to other historical mismeasurement problems.
insights.locator: We use large language models to extract product information

themes.theme: Growth was understated for a century

insights.claim: Using the new quality-adjusted index, real goods consumption in the US grew by a factor of 39 between 1900 and 1990, compared with only a factor of 10.3 using standard goods deflators -- nearly four times more measured growth.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Implies conventional 20th-century US growth and living-standards statistics are significantly understated, which should inform any long-run growth or inflation-history modeling.
insights.locator: real goods consumption grew by a factor of 39

insights.claim: The new index implies substantially lower true goods inflation than conventional deflators throughout the twentieth century.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Challenges the reliability of official historical CPI/deflator series as a true cost-of-living measure over long horizons.
insights.locator: implies substantially lower goods inflation than conventional deflators

insights.claim: The gap between the new index and conventional deflators is largest before World War II, reversing the conventional view that goods consumption growth was slower before 1945 than in the postwar decades.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Overturns a standard economic-history narrative; suggests pre-WWII US growth performance was much stronger than textbooks currently show, a reframing relevant to long-run growth comparisons.
insights.locator: The gap between our index and canonical ones is largest

insights.claim: Tyler Cowen flags this LLM-based historical re-measurement work as important.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A signal from a prominent economist that LLM-driven re-analysis of historical economic data is a research direction worth watching and potentially funding.
insights.quote: Here is a useful tweet storm on the paper, important work.
